On NMT Search Errors and Model Errors: Cat Got Your Tongue?

Felix Stahlberg ∗ and Bill Byrne University of Cambridge Department of Engineering Trumpington St, Cambridge CB2 1PZ, UK { fs439,wjb31 } @cam.ac.uk

Abstract

We report on search errors and model errors in neural machine translation (NMT).

4f348ffc-88f4-49b0-9ebb-5e37181c7476.label: text_chunk
4f348ffc-88f4-49b0-9ebb-5e37181c7476.text:
We present an exact inference procedure for neural se- quence models based on a combination of beam search and depth-first search. We use our exact search to find the global best model scores under a Transformer base model for the entire WMT15 English-German test set.

 Sur- prisingly, beam search fails to find these global best model scores in most cases, even with a very large beam size of 100.

53d52082-82e2-4c63-993e-1875cf0c59c0.label: text_chunk
53d52082-82e2-4c63-993e-1875cf0c59c0.text:
For more than 50% of the sentences, the model in fact assigns its global best score to the empty translation, revealing a massive failure of neural models in properly accounting for adequacy.

 We show by constraining search with a minimum trans- lation length that at the root of the problem of empty translations lies an inherent bias to- wards shorter translations.

6adb8ec2-10dd-4ec9-8284-19f04edf1f84.label: text_chunk
6adb8ec2-10dd-4ec9-8284-19f04edf1f84.text:
We conclude that vanilla NMT in its current form requires just the right amount of beam search errors, which, from a modelling perspective, is a highly un- satisfactory conclusion indeed, as the model often prefers an empty translation.

fd14c111-334e-4148-9678-803616cf3f41.text: To the best of our knowledge, none of the previous studies were able to quantify the number of search errors in un- constrained NMT due to the lack of an exact infer- ence scheme that – although too slow for practi- cal MT – guarantees to find the global best model score for analysis purposes.

4ab6e41b-a739-459d-aa0a-7a4ef13e631f.label: text_chunk
4ab6e41b-a739-459d-aa0a-7a4ef13e631f.text: In this work we propose such an exact decod- ing algorithm for NMT that exploits the mono- tonicity of NMT scores: Since the conditional log-probabilities in Eq. 1 are always negative, partial hypotheses can be safely discarded once their score drops below the log-probability of any complete hypothesis. Using our exact inference scheme we show that beam search does not find the global best model score for more than half of the sentences.

125a44ca-a8a7-4407-9383-07e52b3a3218.label: text_chunk
125a44ca-a8a7-4407-9383-07e52b3a3218.text: However, these search errors, para- doxically, often prevent the decoder from suffer- ing from a frequent but very serious model error in NMT, namely that the empty hypothesis often gets the global best model score. Our findings suggest a reassessment of the amount of model and search errors in NMT, and we hope that they will spark new efforts in improving NMT modeling capabil- ities, especially in terms of adequacy.* Now at Google.

2 Exact Inference for Neural Models

Decoding in NMT (Eq. 2 ) is usually tackled with beam search, which is a time-synchronous approx- imate search algorithm that builds up hypotheses from left to right. A formal algorithm description is given in Alg. 1 . Beam search maintains a set of active hypotheses H cur .

1a626cdf-3301-48a6-a4e0-f8adfa23ef08.label: text_chunk
1a626cdf-3301-48a6-a4e0-f8adfa23ef08.text:
In each iteration, all hypotheses in H cur that do not end with the end- of-sentence symbol < / s > are expanded and col- lected in H next .

 The best n items in H next consti- tute the set of active hypotheses H cur in the next iteration (line 11 in Alg. 1 ), where n is the beam
size.

0a25f799-9496-41d6-894d-e6616f374715.label: text_chunk
0a25f799-9496-41d6-894d-e6616f374715.text:
The algorithm terminates when the best hy- pothesis in H cur ends with the end-of-sentence symbol < / s > . Hypotheses are called complete if they end with < / s > and partial if they do not.

Beam search is the ubiquitous decoding algo- rithm for NMT, but it is prone to search errors as the number of active hypotheses is limited by n . In particular, beam search never compares partial hypotheses of different lengths with each other.

f9dc6040-f74d-45fa-a0c7-131b9ca6a45f.label: text_chunk
f9dc6040-f74d-45fa-a0c7-131b9ca6a45f.text:
As we will see in later sections, this is one of the main sources of search errors. How- ever, in many cases, the model score found by beam search is a reasonable approximation to the global best model score.

 Let γ be the model score found by beam search ( ˜ p in line 12, Alg. 1 ), which is a lower bound on the global best model score: γ ≤ log P (ˆ y | x ) .

To find out more about the length deficiency we constrained exact search to certain translation lengths.

5 Related Work

Other researchers have also noted that large beam sizes yield shorter translations ( Koehn and Knowles , 2017 ). Sountsov and Sarawagi ( 2016 ) argue that this model error is due to the locally nor- malized maximum likelihood training objective in NMT that underestimates the margin between the correct translation and shorter ones if trained with regularization and finite data.

f3fc5fe3-2ef4-415e-ab0a-ef25fe17b72f.label: text_chunk
f3fc5fe3-2ef4-415e-ab0a-ef25fe17b72f.text: A similar argu- ment was made by Murray and Chiang ( 2018 ) who pointed out the difficulty for a locally normalized model to estimate the “budget” for all remaining (longer) translations. Kumar and Sarawagi ( 2019 ) demonstrated that NMT models are often poorly calibrated, and that that can cause the length defi- ciency. Ott et al.

6 Conclusion

We have presented an exact inference scheme for NMT. Exact search may not be practical, but it al- lowed us to discover deficiencies in widely used NMT models. We linked deteriorating BLEU scores of large beams with the reduction of search errors and showed that the model often prefers the empty translation – an evidence of NMT’s failure to properly model adequacy.

2119d050-1416-4265-bbc3-5f7cf469ecbd.text:
Our investigations into length constrained exact search suggested that simple heuristics like length normalization are un- likely to remedy the problem satisfactorily.

**Summary of Relationships Involving Neural Machine Translation (NMT)**

1. **NMT and Probability**: NMT defines the probability P(y|x), which is essential for translating text from one language to another.

2. **NMT and Inference Problem**: The inference problem is crucial in NMT, as it involves decoding the most probable translation for a given input.

3. **NMT as a Subset of Machine Translation (MT)**: NMT is a specialized form of Machine Translation, indicating its specific application within the broader field.

4. **NMT and Beam Search**: NMT is related to beam search, a technique that enhances translation efficiency by exploring multiple hypotheses simultaneously.

5. **NMT Development by Google**: Google plays a significant role in the development and enhancement of NMT technologies, showcasing the organization's investment in this area.

6. **NMT and Length Normalization**: Length normalization is a technique applied within NMT that influences its performance, highlighting its importance in translation quality.

7. **Evaluation of NMT Performance**: The quality of translations produced by NMT systems is often assessed using the BLEU Ratio metric, which serves as a standard evaluation tool.

8. **NMT Dependency on Greedy Algorithms**: NMT relies on search algorithms, such as Greedy, to evaluate translation outputs, indicating its dependence on these methods for scoring translations based on search errors.

This summary encapsulates the key relationships and concepts associated with Neural Machine Translation, emphasizing its definitions, techniques, dependencies, and evaluation methods.
